item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Absolutely incredible. I love the idea. +1. But to be helpful, I have two small criticisms:
"SCP-3268 also varies heavily in size, usually depending on the device it is taking a residence in at the moment. The smallest recorded sighting being approximately 0.5 millimeters in diameter, and the largest being 5 meters in diameter"
This wording is confusing, but I am assuming you mean that the image does not matter, it will generally have the same amount of pixels when spawned?
Also, I would put footnotes for the scientific name of those animals which have the common name in them. I don't want to have to search for each scientific animal name to know which animal it is talking about.
Other than that, this was on point and very good of an idea. Great job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this falls under "Cold, Not Cruel." This is a living creature. It happens to live in the world of the digital, yes, but it's still alive. If we had a dog that could only eat guitars that had been used to play "Norwegian Wood," then we'd get someone with a bit of musical skill to prepare its dinner. The Foundation isn't going to starve something that can be fed for free in about a minute or so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoting on potential. I think it needs a bit more than it has, but I like what it has well enough. I don't know if I'd need a narrative, but I'd really like to have a bit more of an idea of its specific behaviors. I like things that are just an anomalous thing, and honestly I liked it even more when it seemed like it was just somehow a predator that had evolved to function on computers, but I still wanted to know how EXACTLY it worked.

Honestly as it is, the thing I can't help feeling is that it's not too clear how this thing is anomalous. It could just be a really advanced bit of image manipulating software. Sure, way more advanced than anything that actually exists today, but give it a decade or so. The only anomaly seems to be that it's able to tell if an animal's alive or not, and a very skilled observer could usually do the same thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Read it again after the improvements and it changed my mind

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoting because it has potential, but like the other people are saying, needs to be expanded on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Author, I've cleared the tags. If you're unfamiliar with the type of tags used by the SCP wiki, it's best to just leave the page untagged and wait for an experienced tagger to take care of it. We have a team of people on the site who tag articles, and they search for pages that have no tags. Adding only a few tags prevents the team from finding the article, so if you're unfamiliar with the tags, it's totally fine to wait for them to take care of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll work on it some more and see what I can make of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gonna go with what other people said, this is a really neat idea, but it needs to be expanded on. You can't just have a thing that does a thing and give no story behind it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The way I see it is if not keeping it satisfied jeopardizes containment then it’s best to just keep it satisfied. Plus it’s not really adhering to it as much as it is providing basic needs for it like you would to an animal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good idea, the execution leaves a bit to be desired though, I love the idea of it feeding on pictures of animals. I feel like there should be more of a story there though, where did it come from? What made it?

All in all a good article, but perhaps some expanding is needed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Do you think an interview log would help convey the feeling you mentioned?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This really is a good idea, but I think that the idea of "it must be kept satisfied" goes against the idea of the SCP Foundation. It's supposed to contain them, not adhere to them. I dunno, maybe that's just me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The recovery log is pretty over ecumbered with expunged data. You should only censor stuff that can be used against the foundation, for example, instead of writing "June 7th, XXXX" it could be "June 7th, 19XX", to at least give the reader a clue of when it happened, because if not there is no point on writing the year on the first place.
The changes you made are really helpful to the article, but Rainbowknishes is right when he says there is a story missing. There is still hope for this SCP nevertheless, so keep up the good changes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This idea is interesting, but there’s really no narrative.

If you look at newer articles (mainly Series 3 and 4), you’ll see that each one is not just a document on an anomalous thing, but also a self-contained story. Where did this come from? What stories can be told about this SCP? Another trait that can make an article successful is if it invokes a certain emotion in the reader. Do you want the reader to feel sadness? Humor? Fear? It’s even better if your article does both of these things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the feedback, any specific sentences you think I should fix? I fixed a few already but I was wondering if you caught some more.

Also I definitely will be adding some more test logs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
—I think this is a pretty good idea, but its difficult to make a virtual SCP with all the other similar ones around.
Some "unprofesional" words here and there, like calling food to the images given to it. And some more testing logs could make the article much better.

But it needs some polishing for sure. Neutral vote from me.— Just rechecked. Dude, you've won my +1.
